Therefore ’tis certain he was not ambitious.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: If it be found so, some will dear abide it.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: Poor soul! His eyes are red as fire with weeping.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: There’s not a nobler man in Rome than Antony.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: Now mark him. He begins again to speak.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
But yesterday the word of Caesar might
Have stood against the world. Now lies he there,
And none so poor to do him reverence.
O masters, if I were disposed to stir
Your hearts and minds to mutiny and rage,
I should do Brutus wrong, and Cassius wrong—
Who, you all know, are honorable men.
I will not do them wrong. I rather choose
To wrong the dead, to wrong myself and you,
Than I will wrong such honorable men.
But here’s a parchment with the seal of Caesar.
I found it in his closet. 'Tis his will.
Let but the commons hear this testament—
Which, pardon me, I do not mean to read—
And they would go and kiss dead Caesar’s wounds
And dip their napkins in his sacred blood,
Yea, beg a hair of him for memory,
And, dying, mention it within their wills,
Bequeathing it as a rich legacy
Unto their issue.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: We’ll hear the will. Read it, Mark Antony!

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: The will, the will! We will hear Caesar’s will.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Have patience, gentle friends. I must not read it.
It is not meet you know how Caesar loved you.
You are not wood, you are not stones, but men.
And, being men, bearing the will of Caesar,
It will inflame you, it will make you mad.
'Tis good you know not that you are his heirs.
For, if you should—Oh, what would come of it!

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text:
Read the will. We’ll hear it, Antony.
You shall read us the will, Caesar’s will.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Will you be patient? Will you stay awhile?
I have o'ershot myself to tell you of it.
I fear I wrong the honorable men
Whose daggers have stabbed Caesar. I do fear it.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: They were traitors! “Honorable men”!

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: The will! The testament!

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: They were villains, murderers. The will! Read the will!

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
You will compel me, then, to read the will?
Then make a ring about the corpse of Caesar,
And let me show you him that made the will.
Shall I descend? And will you give me leave?

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Come down.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: Descend.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: You shall have leave.
ANTONY comes down from the platform.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text:
A ring!
Stand round.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: Stand from the hearse. Stand from the body.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: Room for Antony, most noble Antony!

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Nay, press not so upon me. Stand far off.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Stand back. Room! Bear back.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
If you have tears, prepare to shed them now.
You all do know this mantle. I remember
The first time ever Caesar put it on.
'Twas on a summer’s evening in his tent,
That day he overcame the Nervii.
Look, in this place ran Cassius' dagger through.
See what a rent the envious Casca made.
Through this the well-belovèd Brutus stabbed.
And as he plucked his cursèd steel away,
Mark how the blood of Caesar followed it,
As rushing out of doors, to be resolved
If Brutus so unkindly knocked, or no.
For Brutus, as you know, was Caesar’s angel.
Judge, O you gods, how dearly Caesar loved him!
This was the most unkindest cut of all.
For when the noble Caesar saw him stab,
Ingratitude, more strong than traitors' arms,
Quite vanquished him. Then burst his mighty heart,
And, in his mantle muffling up his face,
Even at the base of Pompey’s statue,
Which all the while ran blood, great Caesar fell.
O, what a fall was there, my countrymen!
Then I, and you, and all of us fell down,
Whilst bloody treason flourished over us.
Oh, now you weep, and, I perceive, you feel
The dint of pity. These are gracious drops.
Kind souls, what, weep you when you but behold
Our Caesar’s vesture wounded? Look you here,
Here is himself, marred, as you see, with traitors.
[lifts up CAESAR's mantle]

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: O piteous spectacle!

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: O noble Caesar!

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: O woeful day!

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: O traitors, villains!

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: O most bloody sight!

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: We will be revenged.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text:
Revenge! About! Seek! Burn! Fire! Kill! Slay!
Let not a traitor live!

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Stay, countrymen.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: Peace there! Hear the noble Antony.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: We’ll hear him. We’ll follow him. We’ll die with him.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Good friends, sweet friends! Let me not stir you up
To such a sudden flood of mutiny.
They that have done this deed are honorable.
What private griefs they have, alas, I know not,
That made them do it. They are wise and honorable,
And will, no doubt, with reasons answer you.
I come not, friends, to steal away your hearts.
I am no orator, as Brutus is,
But, as you know me all, a plain blunt man
That love my friend. And that they know full well
That gave me public leave to speak of him.
For I have neither wit nor words nor worth,
Action nor utterance nor the power of speech,
To stir men’s blood. I only speak right on.
I tell you that which you yourselves do know,
Show you sweet Caesar’s wounds, poor poor dumb mouths,
And bid them speak for me. But were I Brutus,
And Brutus Antony, there were an Antony
Would ruffle up your spirits and put a tongue
In every wound of Caesar that should move
The stones of Rome to rise and mutiny.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: We’ll mutiny.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: We’ll burn the house of Brutus.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: Away, then! Come, seek the conspirators.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Yet hear me, countrymen. Yet hear me speak.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Peace, ho! Hear Antony. Most noble Antony!

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Why, friends, you go to do you know not what.
Wherein hath Caesar thus deserved your loves?
Alas, you know not. I must tell you then.
You have forgot the will I told you of.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Most true. The will! Let’s stay and hear the will.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Here is the will, and under Caesar’s seal
To every Roman citizen he gives—
To every several man—seventy-five drachmas.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: Most noble Caesar! We’ll revenge his death.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: O royal Caesar!

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Hear me with patience.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Peace, ho!

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Moreover, he hath left you all his walks,
His private arbors and new-planted orchards,
On this side Tiber. He hath left them you
And to your heirs forever—common pleasures,
To walk abroad and recreate yourselves.
Here was a Caesar! When comes such another?

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text:
Never, never.—Come, away, away!
We’ll burn his body in the holy place,
And with the brands fire the traitors' houses.
Take up the body.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: Go fetch fire.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: Pluck down benches.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: Pluck down forms, windows, anything.
PLEBEIANS exit with CAESAR’s body.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Now let it work. Mischief, thou art afoot.
Take thou what course thou wilt!
OCTAVIUS' SERVANT enters.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: How now, fellow?

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS SERVANT
units.text: Sir, Octavius is already come to Rome.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Where is he?

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS SERVANT
units.text: He and Lepidus are at Caesar’s house.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
And thither will I straight to visit him.
He comes upon a wish. Fortune is merry,
And in this mood will give us anything.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS SERVANT
units.text:
I heard him say, Brutus and Cassius
Are rid like madmen through the gates of Rome.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Belike they had some notice of the people
How I had moved them. Bring me to Octavius.
They exit.
CINNA THE POET enters, followed by PLEBEIANS.

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text:
I dreamt tonight that I did feast with Caesar,
And things unlucky charge my fantasy.
I have no will to wander forth of doors,
Yet something leads me forth.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: What is your name?

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: Whither are you going?

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: Where do you dwell?

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: Are you a married man or a bachelor?

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: Answer every man directly.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: Ay, and briefly.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: Ay, and wisely.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: Ay, and truly, you were best.

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text:
What is my name? Whither am I going? Where do I dwell?
Am I a married man or a bachelor? Then, to answer every
man directly and briefly, wisely and truly—wisely I say,
I am a bachelor.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text:
That’s as much as to say they are fools that marry.
You’ll bear me a bang for that, I fear. Proceed,
directly.

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text: Directly, I am going to Caesar’s funeral.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: As a friend or an enemy?

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text: As a friend.

units.speaker: SECOND PLEBEIAN
units.text: That matter is answered directly.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text: For your dwelling—briefly.

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text: Briefly, I dwell by the Capitol.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text: Your name, sir, truly.

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text: Truly, my name is Cinna.

units.speaker: FIRST PLEBEIAN
units.text: Tear him to pieces. He’s a conspirator.

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text: I am Cinna the poet. I am Cinna the poet.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text:
Tear him for his bad verses! Tear him for his bad
verses!

units.speaker: CINNA THE POET
units.text: I am not Cinna the conspirator.

units.speaker: FOURTH PLEBEIAN
units.text:
It is no matter. His name’s Cinna. Pluck but his name
out of his heart and turn him going.

units.speaker: THIRD PLEBEIAN
units.text:
Tear him, tear him! Come, brands, ho! Firebrands: to
Brutus', to Cassius', burn all. Some to Decius' house
and some to Casca’s. Some to Ligarius'. Away, go!
The PLEBEIANS exit, dragging CINNA THE POET.
ANTONY, OCTAVIUS, and LEPIDUS enter.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: These many, then, shall die. Their names are pricked.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
[to LEPIDUS]
Your brother too must die. Consent you, Lepidus?

units.speaker: LEPIDUS
units.text: I do consent—

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: Prick him down, Antony.

units.speaker: LEPIDUS
units.text:
Upon condition Publius shall not live,
Who is your sister’s son, Mark Antony.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
He shall not live. Look, with a spot I damn him.
But, Lepidus, go you to Caesar’s house.
Fetch the will hither, and we shall determine
How to cut off some charge in legacies.

units.speaker: LEPIDUS
units.text: What, shall I find you here?

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: Or here, or at the Capitol.
LEPIDUS exits.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
This is a slight, unmeritable man,
Meet to be sent on errands. Is it fit,
The threefold world divided, he should stand
One of the three to share it?

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
So you thought him.
And took his voice who should be pricked to die
In our black sentence and proscription.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Octavius, I have seen more days than you.
And though we lay these honors on this man
To ease ourselves of divers slanderous loads,
He shall but bear them as the ass bears gold,
To groan and sweat under the business,
Either led or driven, as we point the way.
And having brought our treasure where we will,
Then take we down his load and turn him off,
Like to the empty ass, to shake his ears
And graze in commons.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
You may do your will,
But he’s a tried and valiant soldier.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
So is my horse, Octavius, and for that
I do appoint him store of provender.
It is a creature that I teach to fight,
To wind, to stop, to run directly on,
His corporal motion governed by my spirit,
And, in some taste, is Lepidus but so.
He must be taught and trained and bid go forth,
A barren-spirited fellow, one that feeds
On objects, arts, and imitations,
Which, out of use and staled by other men,
Begin his fashion. Do not talk of him
But as a property. And now, Octavius,
Listen great things. Brutus and Cassius
Are levying powers. We must straight make head.
Therefore let our alliance be combined,
Our best friends made, our means stretched.
And let us presently go sit in council
How covert matters may be best disclosed,
And open perils surest answered.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
Let us do so. For we are at the stake
And bayed about with many enemies.
And some that smile have in their hearts, I fear,
Millions of mischiefs.
They exit.
A drum plays. BRUTUS, LUCIUS, LUCILLIUS, and soldiers enter. TITINIUS and PINDARUS meet them.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Stand, ho!

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text: Give the word, ho, and stand.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: What now, Lucillius? Is Cassius near?

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
He is at hand, and Pindarus is come
To do you salutation from his master.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
He greets me well. —Your master, Pindarus,
In his own change or by ill officers
Hath given me some worthy cause to wish
Things done, undone. But if he be at hand
I shall be satisfied.

units.speaker: PINDARUS
units.text:
I do not doubt
But that my noble master will appear
Such as he is, full of regard and honor.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
He is not doubted. —A word, Lucillius.
[takes LUCILLIUS aside]
How he received you, let me be resolved.

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
With courtesy and with respect enough.
But not with such familiar instances
Nor with such free and friendly conference
As he hath used of old.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Thou hast described
A hot friend cooling. Ever note, Lucillius,
When love begins to sicken and decay,
It useth an enforcèd ceremony.
There are no tricks in plain and simple faith.
But hollow men, like horses hot at hand,
Make gallant show and promise of their mettle.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
But when they should endure the bloody spur,
They fall their crests and, like deceitful jades,
Sink in the trial. Comes his army on?

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
They mean this night in Sardis to be quartered.
The greater part, the horse in general,
Are come with Cassius.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Hark! He is arrived.
March gently on to meet him.
CASSIUS enters with his army.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Stand, ho!

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Stand, ho! Speak the word along.

units.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
units.text: Stand!

units.speaker: SECOND SOLDIER
units.text: Stand!

units.speaker: THIRD SOLDIER
units.text: Stand!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Most noble brother, you have done me wrong.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Judge me, you gods! Wrong I mine enemies?
And if not so, how should I wrong a brother?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Brutus, this sober form of yours hides wrongs.
And when you do them—

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Cassius, be content.
Speak your griefs softly. I do know you well.
Before the eyes of both our armies here,
Which should perceive nothing but love from us,
Let us not wrangle. Bid them move away.
Then in my tent, Cassius, enlarge your griefs,
And I will give you audience.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Pindarus,
Bid our commanders lead their charges off
A little from this ground.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Lucillius, do you the like. And let no man
Come to our tent till we have done our conference.
Let Lucius and Titinius guard our door.
Everyone exits except BRUTUS and CASSIUS.
BRUTUS and CASSIUS remain onstage, now in Brutus' tent.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
That you have wronged me doth appear in this:
You have condemned and noted Lucius Pella
For taking bribes here of the Sardians,
Wherein my letters, praying on his side
Because I knew the man, were slighted off.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: You wronged yourself to write in such a case.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
In such a time as this it is not meet
That every nice offense should bear his comment.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Let me tell you, Cassius, you yourself
Are much condemned to have an itching palm,
To sell and mart your offices for gold
To undeservers.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
I “an itching palm!”
You know that you are Brutus that speak this,
Or, by the gods, this speech were else your last.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
The name of Cassius honors this corruption,
And chastisement doth therefore hide his head.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Chastisement!

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Remember March, the ides of March remember.
Did not great Julius bleed for justice' sake?
What villain touched his body, that did stab,
And not for justice? What, shall one of us
That struck the foremost man of all this world
But for supporting robbers, shall we now
Contaminate our fingers with base bribes,
And sell the mighty space of our large honors
For so much trash as may be graspèd thus?
I had rather be a dog and bay the moon
Than such a Roman.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Brutus, bait not me.
I’ll not endure it. You forget yourself
To hedge me in. I am a soldier, I,
Older in practice, abler than yourself
To make conditions.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Go to. You are not, Cassius.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: I am.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: I say you are not.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Urge me no more, I shall forget myself.
Have mind upon your health, tempt me no further.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Away, slight man!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Is ’t possible?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Hear me, for I will speak.
Must I give way and room to your rash choler?
Shall I be frighted when a madman stares?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: O ye gods, ye gods, must I endure all this?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
“All this?” Ay, more. Fret till your proud heart break.
Go show your slaves how choleric you are
And make your bondmen tremble. Must I budge?
Must I observe you? Must I stand and crouch
Under your testy humor? By the gods,
You shall digest the venom of your spleen,
Though it do split you. For from this day forth,
I’ll use you for my mirth, yea, for my laughter,
When you are waspish.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Is it come to this?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
You say you are a better soldier.
Let it appear so. Make your vaunting true,
And it shall please me well. For mine own part,
I shall be glad to learn of noble men.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
You wrong me every way. You wrong me, Brutus.
I said an elder soldier, not a better.
Did I say “better?”

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: If you did, I care not.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: When Caesar lived, he durst not thus have moved me.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Peace, peace! You durst not so have tempted him.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: I durst not!

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: No.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: What, durst not tempt him?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: For your life you durst not!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Do not presume too much upon my love.
I may do that I shall be sorry for.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
You have done that you should be sorry for.
There is no terror, Cassius, in your threats,
For I am armed so strong in honesty
That they pass by me as the idle wind,
Which I respect not. I did send to you
For certain sums of gold, which you denied me,
For I can raise no money by vile means.
By heaven, I had rather coin my heart
And drop my blood for drachmas than to wring
From the hard hands of peasants their vile trash
By any indirection. I did send
To you for gold to pay my legions,
Which you denied me. Was that done like Cassius?
Should I have answered Caius Cassius so?
When Marcus Brutus grows so covetous
To lock such rascal counters from his friends,
Be ready, gods, with all your thunderbolts.
Dash him to pieces!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: I denied you not.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: You did.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
I did not. He was but a fool that brought
My answer back. Brutus hath rived my heart.
A friend should bear his friend’s infirmities,
But Brutus makes mine greater than they are.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: I do not, till you practice them on me.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: You love me not.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: I do not like your faults.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: A friendly eye could never see such faults.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
A flatterer’s would not, though they do appear
As huge as high Olympus.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Come, Antony, and young Octavius, come,
Revenge yourselves alone on Cassius,
For Cassius is aweary of the world—
Hated by one he loves; braved by his brother;
Checked like a bondman, all his faults observed,
Set in a notebook, learned, and conned by rote
To cast into my teeth. Oh, I could weep
My spirit from mine eyes.
[offers BRUTUS his bared dagger] There is my dagger.
And here my naked breast. Within, a heart
Dearer than Plutus' mine, richer than gold.
If that thou beest a Roman, take it forth.
I, that denied thee gold, will give my heart.
Strike, as thou didst at Caesar. For I know
When thou didst hate him worst, thou lovedst him better
Than ever thou lovedst Cassius.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Sheathe your dagger.
Be angry when you will, it shall have scope.
Do what you will, dishonor shall be humor.
O Cassius, you are yokèd with a lamb
That carries anger as the flint bears fire,
Who, much enforcèd, shows a hasty spark
And straight is cold again.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Hath Cassius lived
To be but mirth and laughter to his Brutus,
When grief and blood ill-tempered vexeth him?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: When I spoke that, I was ill-tempered too.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Do you confess so much? Give me your hand.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: And my heart too.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: O Brutus!

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: What’s the matter?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Have not you love enough to bear with me,
When that rash humor which my mother gave me
Makes me forgetful?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Yes, Cassius. And from henceforth
When you are over-earnest with your Brutus,
He’ll think your mother chides and leave you so.

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
[within] Let me go in to see the generals.
There is some grudge between 'em. 'Tis not meet
They be alone.

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text: [within] You shall not come to them.

units.speaker: POET
units.text: [within] Nothing but death shall stay me.
A POET enters, followed by LUCILLIUS and TITINIUS.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: How now? What’s the matter?

units.speaker: POET
units.text:
For shame, you generals! What do you mean?
Love, and be friends as two such men should be.
For I have seen more years, I’m sure, than ye.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Ha, ha, how vilely doth this cynic rhyme!

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: [to POET] Get you hence, sirrah. Saucy fellow, hence!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Bear with him, Brutus. 'Tis his fashion.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
I’ll know his humor when he knows his time.
What should the wars do with these jigging fools?
—Companion, hence!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Away, away, be gone.
The POET exits.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Lucillius and Titinius, bid the commanders
Prepare to lodge their companies tonight.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
And come yourselves, and bring Messala with you,
Immediately to us.
LUCILLIUS and TITINIUS exit.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: [calls off] Lucius, a bowl of wine!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: I did not think you could have been so angry.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: O Cassius, I am sick of many griefs.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Of your philosophy you make no use
If you give place to accidental evils.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: No man bears sorrow better. Portia is dead.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Ha, Portia?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: She is dead.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
How ’scaped I killing when I crossed you so?
O insupportable and touching loss!
Upon what sickness?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Impatient of my absence,
And grief that young Octavius with Mark Antony
Have made themselves so strong—for with her death
That tidings came—with this she fell distract
And, her attendants absent, swallowed fire.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: And died so?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Even so.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: O ye immortal gods!
LUCIUS enters with wine and candles.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Speak no more of her.—Give me a bowl of wine.—
In this I bury all unkindness, Cassius.
[drinks]

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
My heart is thirsty for that noble pledge.
Fill, Lucius, till the wine o'erswell the cup.
I cannot drink too much of Brutus' love.
[drinks]
LUCIUS exits.
TITINIUS and MESSALA enter.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Come in, Titinius.—Welcome, good Messala!
Now sit we close about this taper here
And call in question our necessities.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Portia, art thou gone?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
No more, I pray you.
—Messala, I have here receivèd letters
That young Octavius and Mark Antony
Come down upon us with a mighty power,
Bending their expedition toward Philippi.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Myself have letters of the selfsame tenor.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: With what addition?

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
That by proscription and bills of outlawry,
Octavius, Antony, and Lepidus
Have put to death an hundred senators.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Therein our letters do not well agree.
Mine speak of seventy senators that died
By their proscriptions, Cicero being one.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Cicero one?

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
Cicero is dead,
And by that order of proscription.
[to BRUTUS] Had you your letters from your wife, my
lord?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: No, Messala.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Nor nothing in your letters writ of her?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Nothing, Messala.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: That methinks is strange.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Why ask you? Hear you aught of her in yours?

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: No, my lord.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Now, as you are a Roman, tell me true.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
Then like a Roman bear the truth I tell.
For certain she is dead, and by strange manner.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Why, farewell, Portia. We must die, Messala.
With meditating that she must die once,
I have the patience to endure it now.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Even so great men great losses should endure.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
I have as much of this in art as you,
But yet my nature could not bear it so.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Well, to our work alive. What do you think
Of marching to Philippi presently?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: I do not think it good.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Your reason?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
This it is:
'Tis better that the enemy seek us.
So shall he waste his means, weary his soldiers,
Doing himself offense, whilst we, lying still,
Are full of rest, defense, and nimbleness.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Good reasons must of force give place to better.
The people ’twixt Philippi and this ground
Do stand but in a forced affection,
For they have grudged us contribution.
The enemy, marching along by them,
By them shall make a fuller number up,
Come on refreshed, new-added, and encouraged,
From which advantage shall we cut him off
If at Philippi we do face him there,
These people at our back.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Hear me, good brother—

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Under your pardon. You must note beside,
That we have tried the utmost of our friends,
Our legions are brim-full, our cause is ripe.
The enemy increaseth every day.
We, at the height, are ready to decline.
There is a tide in the affairs of men,
Which, taken at the flood, leads on to fortune;
Omitted, all the voyage of their life
Is bound in shallows and in miseries.
On such a full sea are we now afloat,
And we must take the current when it serves
Or lose our ventures.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Then, with your will, go on.
We’ll along ourselves, and meet them at Philippi.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
The deep of night is crept upon our talk,
And nature must obey necessity,
Which we will niggard with a little rest.
There is no more to say?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
No more. Good night.
Early tomorrow will we rise and hence.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Lucius!
LUCIUS enters.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: My gown.
LUCIUS exits.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Farewell, good Messala.—
Good night, Titinius.—Noble, noble Cassius,
Good night and good repose.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
O my dear brother,
This was an ill beginning of the night.
Never come such division ’tween our souls.
Let it not, Brutus.
LUCIUS enters with the nightgown.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Everything is well.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Good night, my lord.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Good night, good brother.

units.speaker: TITINIUS MESSALA
units.text: Good night, Lord Brutus.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Farewell, everyone.
CASSIUS, TITINIUS, and MESSALA exit.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Give me the gown. Where is thy instrument?

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Here in the tent.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
What, thou speak’st drowsily?
Poor knave, I blame thee not. Thou art o'erwatched.
Call Claudio and some other of my men.
I’ll have them sleep on cushions in my tent.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Varrus and Claudio!
VARRUS and CLAUDIO enter.

units.speaker: VARRUS
units.text: Calls my lord?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
I pray you, sirs, lie in my tent and sleep.
It may be I shall raise you by and by
On business to my brother Cassius.

units.speaker: VARRUS
units.text: So please you, we will stand and watch your pleasure.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
I will not have it so. Lie down, good sirs.
It may be I shall otherwise bethink me.
—Look, Lucius, here’s the book I sought for so.
I put it in the pocket of my gown.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: I was sure your lordship did not give it me.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Bear with me, good boy, I am much forgetful.
Canst thou hold up thy heavy eyes awhile,
And touch thy instrument a strain or two?

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Ay, my lord, an ’t please you.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
It does, my boy.
I trouble thee too much, but thou art willing.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: It is my duty, sir.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
I should not urge thy duty past thy might.
I know young bloods look for a time of rest.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: I have slept, my lord, already.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
It was well done, and thou shalt sleep again.
I will not hold thee long. If I do live,
I will be good to thee.
LUCIUS plays music and sings a song, then falls asleep.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
This is a sleepy tune. O murderous slumber,
Layst thou thy leaden mace upon my boy
That plays thee music? —Gentle knave, good night.
I will not do thee so much wrong to wake thee.
If thou dost nod, thou break’st thy instrument.
I’ll take it from thee. And, good boy, good night.
—Let me see, let me see. Is not the leaf turned down
Where I left reading? Here it is, I think.
The GHOST of Caesar enters.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
How ill this taper burns!—Ha, who comes here?
I think it is the weakness of mine eyes
That shapes this monstrous apparition.
It comes upon me.—Art thou any thing?
Art thou some god, some angel, or some devil
That makest my blood cold and my hair to stare?
Speak to me what thou art.

units.speaker: GHOST
units.text: Thy evil spirit, Brutus.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Why comest thou?

units.speaker: GHOST
units.text: To tell thee thou shalt see me at Philippi.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Well, then I shall see thee again?

units.speaker: GHOST
units.text: Ay, at Philippi.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Why, I will see thee at Philippi, then.
The GHOST exits.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Now I have taken heart thou vanishest.
Ill spirit, I would hold more talk with thee.
—Boy, Lucius!—Varrus!—Claudio!—Sirs, awake!
—Claudio!

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: The strings, my lord, are false.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
He thinks he still is at his instrument.
Lucius, awake.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: My lord?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Didst thou dream, Lucius, that thou so criedst out?

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: My lord, I do not know that I did cry.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Yes, that thou didst. Didst thou see any thing?

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Nothing, my lord.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Sleep again, Lucius.—Sirrah Claudio!
[to VARRUS]
Fellow thou, awake!

units.speaker: VARRUS
units.text: My lord?

units.speaker: CLAUDIO
units.text: My lord?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Why did you so cry out, sirs, in your sleep?

units.speaker: VARRUS CLAUDIO
units.text: Did we, my lord?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Ay. Saw you anything?

units.speaker: VARRUS
units.text: No, my lord, I saw nothing.

units.speaker: CLAUDIO
units.text: Nor I, my lord.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Go and commend me to my brother Cassius.
Bid him set on his powers betimes before,
And we will follow.

units.speaker: VARRUS CLAUDIO
units.text: It shall be done, my lord.
Everyone exits in different directions.
OCTAVIUS and ANTONY enter, along with their army.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
Now, Antony, our hopes are answerèd.
You said the enemy would not come down
But keep the hills and upper regions.
It proves not so. Their battles are at hand.
They mean to warn us at Philippi here,
Answering before we do demand of them.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Tut, I am in their bosoms, and I know
Wherefore they do it. They could be content
To visit other places, and come down
With fearful bravery, thinking by this face
To fasten in our thoughts that they have courage.
But ’tis not so.
A MESSENGER enters.

units.speaker: MESSENGER
units.text:
Prepare you, generals.
The enemy comes on in gallant show.
Their bloody sign of battle is hung out,
And something to be done immediately.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Octavius, lead your battle softly on,
Upon the left hand of the even field.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: Upon the right hand I. Keep thou the left.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Why do you cross me in this exigent?

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: I do not cross you. But I will do so.
The sound of drums and soldiers. BRUTUS, CASSIUS, and their army enter, including LUCILLIUS, TITINIUS, and MESSALA.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: They stand and would have parley.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Stand fast, Titinius. We must out and talk.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: Mark Antony, shall we give sign of battle?

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
No, Caesar, we will answer on their charge.
Make forth. The generals would have some words.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: [to his army] Stir not until the signal.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Words before blows. Is it so, countrymen?

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: Not that we love words better, as you do.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Good words are better than bad strokes, Octavius.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
In your bad strokes, Brutus, you give good words.
Witness the hole you made in Caesar’s heart,
Crying “Long live, hail, Caesar!”

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Antony,
The posture of your blows are yet unknown.
But for your words, they rob the Hybla bees
And leave them honeyless.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Not stingless too?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Oh, yes, and soundless too.
For you have stol'n their buzzing, Antony,
And very wisely threat before you sting.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
Villains, you did not so when your vile daggers
Hacked one another in the sides of Caesar.
You showed your teeth like apes, and fawned like
hounds,
And bowed like bondmen, kissing Caesar’s feet,
Whilst damnèd Casca, like a cur, behind
Struck Caesar on the neck. O you flatterers!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Flatterers?—Now, Brutus, thank yourself.
This tongue had not offended so today
If Cassius might have ruled.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
Come, come, the cause. If arguing make us sweat,
The proof of it will turn to redder drops.
[draws his sword] Look, I draw a sword against
conspirators.
When think you that the sword goes up again?
Never, till Caesar’s three and thirty wounds
Be well avenged, or till another Caesar
Have added slaughter to the sword of traitors.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Caesar, thou canst not die by traitors' hands
Unless thou bring’st them with thee.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
So I hope.
I was not born to die on Brutus' sword.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
O, if thou wert the noblest of thy strain,
Young man, thou couldst not die more honorable.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
A peevish schoolboy, worthless of such honor,
Joined with a masker and a reveler!

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Old Cassius still.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
Come, Antony, away.—
Defiance, traitors, hurl we in your teeth.
If you dare fight today, come to the field.
If not, when you have stomachs.
OCTAVIUS, ANTONY, and their army exit.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Why, now, blow wind, swell billow, and swim bark!
The storm is up and all is on the hazard.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Ho, Lucillius, hark, a word with you.

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
[stands forth]
My lord?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: Messala!

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
[stands forth]
What says my general?

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Messala,
This is my birthday, as this very day
Was Cassius born. Give me thy hand, Messala.
Be thou my witness that against my will,
As Pompey was, am I compelled to set
Upon one battle all our liberties.
You know that I held Epicurus strong
And his opinion. Now I change my mind,
And partly credit things that do presage.
Coming from Sardis, on our former ensign
Two mighty eagles fell, and there they perched,
Gorging and feeding from our soldiers' hands,
Who to Philippi here consorted us.
This morning are they fled away and gone,
And in their steads do ravens, crows, and kites
Fly o'er our heads and downward look on us
As we were sickly prey. Their shadows seem
A canopy most fatal, under which
Our army lies, ready to give up the ghost.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Believe not so.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
I but believe it partly,
For I am fresh of spirit and resolved
To meet all perils very constantly.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: [returning with LUCILLIUS] Even so, Lucillius.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Now, most noble Brutus,
The gods today stand friendly that we may,
Lovers in peace, lead on our days to age.
But since the affairs of men rest still incertain,
Let’s reason with the worst that may befall.
If we do lose this battle, then is this
The very last time we shall speak together.
What are you then determinèd to do?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Even by the rule of that philosophy
By which I did blame Cato for the death
Which he did give himself — I know not how,
But I do find it cowardly and vile,
For fear of what might fall, so to prevent
The time of life — arming myself with patience
To stay the providence of some high powers
That govern us below.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Then if we lose this battle
You are contented to be led in triumph
Thorough the streets of Rome?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
No, Cassius, no. Think not, thou noble Roman,
That ever Brutus will go bound to Rome.
He bears too great a mind. But this same day
Must end that work the ides of March begun.
And whether we shall meet again I know not.
Therefore our everlasting farewell take.
Forever and forever farewell, Cassius.
If we do meet again, why, we shall smile.
If not, why then this parting was well made.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Forever and forever farewell, Brutus.
If we do meet again, we’ll smile indeed.
If not, ’tis true this parting was well made.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Why then, lead on. Oh, that a man might know
The end of this day’s business ere it come!
But it sufficeth that the day will end,
And then the end is known.—Come, ho! Away!
They all exit.
Sounds of battle. BRUTUS and MESSALA enter.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Ride, ride, Messala, ride, and give these bills
Unto the legions on the other side.
Sounds of battle.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Let them set on at once, for I perceive
But cold demeanor in Octavius' wing,
And sudden push gives them the overthrow.
Ride, ride, Messala. Let them all come down.
They exit in opposite directions.
Sounds of battle. CASSIUS and TITINIUS enter.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
O, look, Titinius, look, the villains fly!
Myself have to mine own turned enemy.
This ensign here of mine was turning back.
I slew the coward and did take it from him.
[indicates his standard]

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text:
O Cassius, Brutus gave the word too early,
Who, having some advantage on Octavius,
Took it too eagerly. His soldiers fell to spoil,
Whilst we by Antony are all enclosed.
PINDARUS enters.

units.speaker: PINDARUS
units.text:
Fly further off, my lord, fly further off.
Mark Antony is in your tents, my lord.
Fly, therefore, noble Cassius, fly far off.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
This hill is far enough.—Look, look, Titinius.
Are those my tents where I perceive the fire?

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text: They are, my lord.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Titinius, if thou lovest me,
Mount thou my horse, and hide thy spurs in him
Till he have brought thee up to yonder troops
And here again, that I may rest assured
Whether yond troops are friend or enemy.

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text: I will be here again, even with a thought.
TITINIUS exits.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Go, Pindarus, get higher on that hill.
My sight was ever thick. Regard Titinius,
And tell me what thou notest about the field.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
This day I breathed first. Time is come round,
And where I did begin, there shall I end.
My life is run his compass.
[to PINDARUS] Sirrah, what news?

units.speaker: PINDARUS
units.text: [above] O my lord!

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text: What news?

units.speaker: PINDARUS
units.text:
[above] Titinius is enclosèd round about
With horsemen, that make to him on the spur.
Yet he spurs on. Now they are almost on him.
Now, Titinius. Now some light. Oh, he lights too.
He’s ta'en.
A shout offstage.

units.speaker: PINDARUS
units.text: And, hark! They shout for joy.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Come down, behold no more.
Oh, coward that I am, to live so long
To see my best friend ta'en before my face!
PINDARUS returns.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Come hither, sirrah.
In Parthia did I take thee prisoner.
And then I swore thee, saving of thy life,
That whatsoever I did bid thee do,
Thou shouldst attempt it. Come now, keep thine oath.
[gives his sword to PINDARUS]
Now be a free man, and with this good sword
That ran through Caesar’s bowels, search this bosom.
Stand not to answer. Here take thou the hilts
And, when my face is covered, as ’tis now,
Guide thou the sword.

units.speaker: CASSIUS
units.text:
Caesar, thou art revenged,
Even with the sword that killed thee.
[dies]

units.speaker: PINDARUS
units.text:
So I am free. Yet would not so have been,
Durst I have done my will. O Cassius,
Far from this country Pindarus shall run,
Where never Roman shall take note of him.
PINDARUS exits.
TITINIUS and MESSALA enter.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
It is but change, Titinius, for Octavius
Is overthrown by noble Brutus' power,
As Cassius' legions are by Antony.

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text: These tidings will well comfort Cassius.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Where did you leave him?

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text:
All disconsolate,
With Pindarus his bondman on this hill.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Is not that he that lies upon the ground?

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text: He lies not like the living. O my heart!

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Is not that he?

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text:
No, this was he, Messala,
But Cassius is no more. O setting sun,
As in thy red rays thou dost sink tonight,
So in his red blood Cassius' day is set.
The sun of Rome is set. Our day is gone.
Clouds, dews, and dangers come! Our deeds are done.
Mistrust of my success hath done this deed.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
Mistrust of good success hath done this deed.
O hateful error, melancholy’s child,
Why dost thou show to the apt thoughts of men
The things that are not? O error, soon conceived,
Thou never comest unto a happy birth
But kill’st the mother that engendered thee!

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text: What, Pindarus! Where art thou, Pindarus?

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
Seek him, Titinius, whilst I go to meet
The noble Brutus, thrusting this report
Into his ears. I may say “thrusting” it,
For piercing steel and darts envenomèd
Shall be as welcome to the ears of Brutus
As tidings of this sight.

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text:
Hie you, Messala,
And I will seek for Pindarus the while.
MESSALA exits.

units.speaker: TITINIUS
units.text:
Why didst thou send me forth, brave Cassius?
Did I not meet thy friends? And did not they
Put on my brows this wreath of victory
And bid me give it thee? Didst thou not hear their
shouts?
Alas, thou hast misconstrued everything!
But, hold thee, take this garland on thy brow.
Thy Brutus bid me give it thee, and I
Will do his bidding.
[lays wreath on CASSIUS’ head] Brutus, come apace,
And see how I regarded Caius Cassius.
—By your leave, gods, this is a Roman’s part.
Come, Cassius' sword, and find Titinius' heart.
[stabs himself with CASSIUS’s sword and dies]
Sounds of battle. BRUTUS, MESSALA, Young CATO, STRATO, VOLUMNIUS, LUCILLIUS, LABIO, and FLAVIO enter.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Where, where, Messala, doth his body lie?

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: Lo, yonder, and Titinius mourning it.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Titinius' face is upward.

units.speaker: CATO
units.text: He is slain.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
O Julius Caesar, thou art mighty yet!
Thy spirit walks abroad and turns our swords
In our own proper entrails.

units.speaker: CATO
units.text:
Brave Titinius!—
Look whe 'er he have not crowned dead Cassius.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Are yet two Romans living such as these?
—The last of all the Romans, fare thee well!
It is impossible that ever Rome
Should breed thy fellow. —Friends, I owe more tears
To this dead man than you shall see me pay.
—I shall find time, Cassius, I shall find time.
—Come, therefore, and to Thasos send his body.
His funerals shall not be in our camp,
Lest it discomfort us. —Lucillius, come. —
And come, young Cato. Let us to the field.
—Labio and Flavio, set our battles on.
—'Tis three o'clock, and, Romans, yet ere night
We shall try fortune in a second fight.
They all exit.
Sounds of battle. BRUTUS, MESSALA, Young CATO, LUCILLIUS, and FLAVIO enter.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Yet, countrymen, O, yet hold up your heads!
BRUTUS, MESSALA, and FLAVIO exit.

units.speaker: CATO
units.text:
What bastard doth not? Who will go with me?
I will proclaim my name about the field.
I am the son of Marcus Cato, ho!
A foe to tyrants, and my country’s friend.
I am the son of Marcus Cato, ho!
ANTONY and OCTAVIUS' SOLDIERS enter and fight.

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
And I am Brutus, Marcus Brutus, I!
Brutus, my country’s friend. Know me for Brutus!

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
O young and noble Cato, art thou down?
Why, now thou diest as bravely as Titinius,
And mayst be honored, being Cato’s son.

units.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
units.text: [to LUCILLIUS] Yield, or thou diest.

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
Only I yield to die.
There is so much that thou wilt kill me straight.
Kill Brutus, and be honored in his death.

units.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
units.text: We must not. A noble prisoner!
ANTONY enters.

units.speaker: SECOND SOLDIER
units.text: Room, ho! Tell Antony Brutus is ta'en.

units.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
units.text:
I’ll tell the news. Here comes the general.
—Brutus is ta'en, Brutus is ta'en, my lord.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text: Where is he?

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
Safe, Antony. Brutus is safe enough.
I dare assure thee that no enemy
Shall ever take alive the noble Brutus.
The gods defend him from so great a shame!
When you do find him, or alive or dead,
He will be found like Brutus, like himself.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
[to SOLDIERS] This is not Brutus, friend, but, I assure
you,
A prize no less in worth. Keep this man safe.
Give him all kindness. I had rather have
Such men my friends than enemies. Go on,
And see whether Brutus be alive or dead.
And bring us word unto Octavius' tent
How everything is chanced.
They exit in opposite directions.
BRUTUS, DARDANIUS, CLITUS, STRATO, and VOLUMNIUS enter.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Come, poor remains of friends, rest on this rock.

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text:
Statilius showed the torchlight but, my lord,
He came not back. He is or ta'en or slain.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Sit thee down, Clitus. Slaying is the word.
It is a deed in fashion. Hark thee, Clitus.
[whispers to CLITUS]

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text: What, I, my lord? No, not for all the world.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Peace then! No words.

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text: I’ll rather kill myself.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Hark thee, Dardanius.
[whispers to DARDANIUS]

units.speaker: DARDANIUS
units.text: Shall I do such a deed?

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text: O Dardanius!

units.speaker: DARDANIUS
units.text: O Clitus!

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text:
[aside to DARDANIUS]
What ill request did Brutus make to thee?

units.speaker: DARDANIUS
units.text:
[aside to CLITUS]
To kill him, Clitus. Look, he meditates.

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text:
[aside to DARDANIUS] Now is that noble vessel full of
grief,
That it runs over even at his eyes.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Come hither, good Volumnius. List a word.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIUS
units.text: What says my lord?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Why this, Volumnius:
The ghost of Caesar hath appeared to me
Two several times by night. At Sardis once,
And this last night here in Philippi fields.
I know my hour is come.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIUS
units.text: Not so, my lord.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Nay, I am sure it is, Volumnius.
Thou seest the world, Volumnius, how it goes.
Our enemies have beat us to the pit.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
It is more worthy to leap in ourselves
Than tarry till they push us. Good Volumnius,
Thou know’st that we two went to school together.
Even for that our love of old, I prithee,
Hold thou my sword hilts, whilst I run on it.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIUS
units.text: That’s not an office for a friend, my lord.
Sounds of battle.

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text: Fly, fly, my lord. There is no tarrying here.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Farewell to you. —And you .—And you, Volumnius.
—Strato, thou hast been all this while asleep.
Farewell to thee too, Strato. —Countrymen,
My heart doth joy that yet in all my life
I found no man but he was true to me.
I shall have glory by this losing day
More than Octavius and Mark Antony
By this vile conquest shall attain unto.
So fare you well at once, for Brutus' tongue
Hath almost ended his life’s history.
Night hangs upon mine eyes. My bones would rest,
That have but labored to attain this hour.

units.speaker: CLITUS
units.text: Fly, my lord, fly.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text: Hence. I will follow.
CLITUS, DARDANIUS, and VOLUMNIUS exit.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
I prithee, Strato, stay thou by thy lord.
Thou art a fellow of a good respect.
Thy life hath had some smatch of honor in it.
Hold then my sword and turn away thy face
While I do run upon it. Wilt thou, Strato?

units.speaker: STRATO
units.text:
Give me your hand first.
[holds BRUTUS' sword] Fare you well, my lord.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
units.text:
Farewell, good Strato.
[runs on his sword] Caesar, now be still.
I killed not thee with half so good a will.
[dies]
Sounds of battle. Trumpets sound a retreat. OCTAVIUS, ANTONY, MESSALA, and LUCILLIUS enter with the army.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: What man is that?

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: My master’s man.—Strato, where is thy master?

units.speaker: STRATO
units.text:
Free from the bondage you are in, Messala.
The conquerors can but make a fire of him.
For Brutus only overcame himself,
And no man else hath honor by his death.

units.speaker: LUCILLIUS
units.text:
So Brutus should be found.—I thank thee, Brutus,
That thou hast proved Lucillius' saying true.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
All that served Brutus, I will entertain them.
—Fellow, wilt thou bestow thy time with me?

units.speaker: STRATO
units.text: Ay, if Messala will prefer me to you.

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text: Do so, good Messala.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text: How died my master, Strato?

units.speaker: STRATO
units.text: I held the sword and he did run on it.

units.speaker: MESSALA
units.text:
Octavius, then take him to follow thee,
That did the latest service to my master.

units.speaker: ANTONY
units.text:
This was the noblest Roman of them all.
All the conspirators save only he
Did that they did in envy of great Caesar.
He only in a general honest thought
And common good to all, made one of them.
His life was gentle, and the elements
So mixed in him that Nature might stand up
And say to all the world, “This was a man.”

units.speaker: OCTAVIUS
units.text:
According to his virtue let us use him,
With all respect and rites of burial.
Within my tent his bones tonight shall lie
Most like a soldier, ordered honorably.
So call the field to rest, and let’s away
To part the glories of this happy day.
All exit.

sentences.text: FLAVIUS and MURELLUS enter on one side of the stage, as do a CARPENTER, a COBBLER, and some other commoners from the other end of the stage.
FLAVIUS and MURELLUS enter on one side of the stage, as do a CARPENTER, a COBBLER, and some other commoners from the other end of the stage.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Hence! Home, you idle creatures get you home!
Is this a holiday? What, know you not,
Being mechanical, you ought not walk
Upon a laboring day without the sign
Of your profession? —Speak, what trade art thou?

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:

Is this a holiday? What, know you not,
Being mechanical, you ought not walk
Upon a laboring day without the sign
Of your profession? —Speak, what trade art thou?

Being mechanical, you ought not walk
Upon a laboring day without the sign

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Hence! Home, you idle creatures get you home!
Is this a holiday? What, know you not,
Being mechanical, you ought not walk
Upon a laboring day without the sign
Of your profession? —Speak, what trade art thou?

sentences.speaker: CARPENTER
sentences.text: Why, sir, a carpenter.

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text:
Where is thy leather apron and thy rule?
What dost thou with thy best apparel on?
—You, sir, what trade are you?

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text:
Where is thy leather apron and thy rule?
What dost thou with thy best apparel on?
—You, sir, what trade are you?

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Truly, sir, in respect of a fine workman, I am but, as
you would say, a cobbler.

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text: But what trade art thou? Answer me directly.

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text: But what trade art thou? Answer me directly.

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
A trade, sir, that I hope I may use with a safe
conscience, which is, indeed, sir, a mender of bad
soles.

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
A trade, sir, that I hope I may use with a safe
conscience, which is, indeed, sir, a mender of bad
soles.

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text: What trade, thou knave? Thou naughty knave, what trade?

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text: What trade, thou knave? Thou naughty knave, what trade?

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Nay, I beseech you, sir, be not out with me. Yet, if
you be out, sir, I can mend you.

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Nay, I beseech you, sir, be not out with me. Yet, if
you be out, sir, I can mend you.

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text:
What mean’st thou by that? “Mend” me, thou saucy
fellow?

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text: Why, sir, cobble you.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text: Thou art a cobbler, art thou?

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Truly, sir, all that I live by is with the awl. I
meddle with no tradesman’s matters nor women’s matters,
but withal I am indeed, sir, a surgeon to old shoes.
When they are in great danger, I recover them. As proper
men as ever trod upon neat’s leather have gone upon my
handiwork.

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Truly, sir, all that I live by is with the awl. I
meddle with no tradesman’s matters nor women’s matters,
but withal I am indeed, sir, a surgeon to old shoes.
When they are in great danger, I recover them. As proper
men as ever trod upon neat’s leather have gone upon my
handiwork.

but withal I am indeed, sir, a surgeon to old shoes.

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Truly, sir, all that I live by is with the awl. I
meddle with no tradesman’s matters nor women’s matters,
but withal I am indeed, sir, a surgeon to old shoes.
When they are in great danger, I recover them. As proper
men as ever trod upon neat’s leather have gone upon my
handiwork.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
But wherefore art not in thy shop today?
Why dost thou lead these men about the streets?

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
But wherefore art not in thy shop today?
Why dost thou lead these men about the streets?

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Truly, sir, to wear out their shoes to get myself into
more work. But indeed, sir, we make holiday to see
Caesar and to rejoice in his triumph.

sentences.speaker: COBBLER
sentences.text:
Truly, sir, to wear out their shoes to get myself into
more work. But indeed, sir, we make holiday to see
Caesar and to rejoice in his triumph.

sentences.speaker: MURELLUS
sentences.text:
Wherefore rejoice? What conquest brings he home?
What tributaries follow him to Rome
To grace in captive bonds his chariot wheels?
You blocks, you stones, you worse than senseless
things,
O you hard hearts, you cruèl men of Rome,
Knew you not Pompey? Many a time and oft
Have you climbed up to walls and battlements,
To towers and windows, yea, to chimney tops,
Your infants in your arms, and there have sat
The livelong day with patient expectation
To see great Pompey pass the streets of Rome.
And when you saw his chariot but appear,
Have you not made an universal shout
That Tiber trembled underneath her banks
To hear the replication of your sounds
Made in her concave shores?
And do you now put on your best attire?
And do you now cull out a holiday?
And do you now strew flowers in his way
That comes in triumph over Pompey’s blood?
Be gone!
Run to your houses, fall upon your knees,
Pray to the gods to intermit the plague
That needs must light on this ingratitude.

To grace in captive bonds his chariot wheels?
You blocks, you stones, you worse than senseless
things,
O you hard hearts, you cruèl men of Rome,
Knew you not Pompey? Many a time and oft
Have you climbed up to walls and battlements,
To towers and windows, yea, to chimney tops,
Your infants in your arms, and there have sat
The livelong day with patient expectation
To see great Pompey pass the streets of Rome.
And when you saw his chariot but appear,
Have you not made an universal shout
That Tiber trembled underneath her banks
To hear the replication of your sounds
Made in her concave shores?
And do you now put on your best attire?
And do you now cull out a holiday?
And do you now strew flowers in his way
That comes in triumph over Pompey’s blood?
Be gone!
Run to your houses, fall upon your knees,
Pray to the gods to intermit the plague
That needs must light on this ingratitude.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Go, go, good countrymen, and for this fault,
Assemble all the poor men of your sort,
Draw them to Tiber banks, and weep your tears
Into the channel till the lowest stream
Do kiss the most exalted shores of all.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
Go, go, good countrymen, and for this fault,
Assemble all the poor men of your sort,
Draw them to Tiber banks, and weep your tears
Into the channel till the lowest stream
Do kiss the most exalted shores of all.
The CARPENTER, COBBLER, and all of the commoners exit.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
See whether their basest metal be not moved.
They vanish tongue-tied in their guiltiness.
Go you down that way towards the Capitol.
This way will I. Disrobe the images
If you do find them decked with ceremonies.

sentences.speaker: FLAVIUS
sentences.text:
It is no matter. Let no images
Be hung with Caesar’s trophies. I’ll about
And drive away the vulgar from the streets.
So do you too, where you perceive them thick.
These growing feathers plucked from Caesar’s wing
Will make him fly an ordinary pitch,
Who else would soar above the view of men
And keep us all in servile fearfulness.

sentences.text: Calphurnia!
Peace, ho! Caesar speaks.
Peace, ho! Caesar speaks.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text: Calphurnia!

sentences.speaker: CALPHURNIA
sentences.text: Here, my lord.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Stand you directly in Antonius' way
When he doth run his course. —Antonius!

sentences.speaker: ANTONY
sentences.text: Caesar, my lord.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
Forget not in your speed, Antonius,
To touch Calphurnia, for our elders say
The barren, touchèd in this holy chase,
Shake off their sterile curse.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR

When Caesar says, “do this,” it is performed.

sentences.speaker: CASCA

sentences.speaker: CAESAR

Who is it in the press that calls on me?
I hear a tongue, shriller than all the music,
Cry “Caesar!” —Speak. Caesar is turned to hear.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text: Will you go see the order of the course?

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text: Not I.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text: I pray you, do.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
I am not gamesome. I do lack some part
Of that quick spirit that is in Antony.
Let me not hinder, Cassius, your desires.
I’ll leave you.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Brutus, I do observe you now of late
I have not from your eyes that gentleness
And show of love as I was wont to have.
You bear too stubborn and too strange a hand
Over your friend that loves you.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Brutus, I do observe you now of late
I have not from your eyes that gentleness

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
Cassius,
Be not deceived. If I have veiled my look,
I turn the trouble of my countenance
Merely upon myself. Vexèd I am
Of late with passions of some difference,
Conceptions only proper to myself,
Which give some soil perhaps to my behaviors.
But let not therefore, my good friends, be grieved—
Among which number, Cassius, be you one—
Nor construe any further my neglect
Than that poor Brutus, with himself at war,
Forgets the shows of love to other men.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Then, Brutus, I have much mistook your passion,
By means whereof this breast of mine hath buried
Thoughts of great value, worthy cogitations.
Tell me, good Brutus, can you see your face?

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Then, Brutus, I have much mistook your passion,
By means whereof this breast of mine hath buried
Thoughts of great value, worthy cogitations.
Tell me, good Brutus, can you see your face?

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
No, Cassius, for the eye sees not itself
But by reflection, by some other things.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
'Tis just.
And it is very much lamented, Brutus,
That you have no such mirrors as will turn
Your hidden worthiness into your eye
That you might see your shadow . I have heard
Where many of the best respect in Rome,
Except immortal Caesar, speaking of Brutus
And groaning underneath this age’s yoke,
Have wished that noble Brutus had his eyes.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
Into what dangers would you lead me, Cassius,
That you would have me seek into myself
For that which is not in me?

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Therefore, good Brutus, be prepared to hear.
And since you know you cannot see yourself
So well as by reflection, I, your glass,
Will modestly discover to yourself
That of yourself which you yet know not of.
And be not jealous on me, gentle Brutus.
Were I a common laugher, or did use
To stale with ordinary oaths my love
To every new protester, if you know
That I do fawn on men and hug them hard
And, after, scandal them, or if you know
That I profess myself in banqueting
To all the rout, then hold me dangerous.

Therefore, good Brutus, be prepared to hear.

So well as by reflection, I, your glass,
Will modestly discover to yourself
That of yourself which you yet know not of.
And be not jealous on me, gentle Brutus.
Were I a common laugher, or did use
To stale with ordinary oaths my love
To every new protester, if you know
That I do fawn on men and hug them hard
And, after, scandal them, or if you know
That I profess myself in banqueting
To all the rout, then hold me dangerous.
Trumpets play, and then a shout sounds.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
What means this shouting? I do fear, the people
Choose Caesar for their king.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
What means this shouting? I do fear, the people
Choose Caesar for their king.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Ay, do you fear it?
Then must I think you would not have it so.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Ay, do you fear it?
Then must I think you would not have it so.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
I would not, Cassius. Yet I love him well.
But wherefore do you hold me here so long?
What is it that you would impart to me?
If it be aught toward the general good,
Set honor in one eye and death i' th' other,
And I will look on both indifferently,
For let the gods so speed me as I love
The name of honor more than I fear death.

And I will look on both indifferently,

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
I know that virtue to be in you, Brutus,
As well as I do know your outward favor.
Well, honor is the subject of my story.
I cannot tell what you and other men
Think of this life, but, for my single self,
I had as lief not be as live to be
In awe of such a thing as I myself.
I was born free as Caesar. So were you.
We both have fed as well, and we can both
Endure the winter’s cold as well as he.
For once upon a raw and gusty day,
The troubled Tiber chafing with her shores,
Caesar said to me, “Darest thou, Cassius, now
Leap in with me into this angry flood
And swim to yonder point?” Upon the word,
Accoutred as I was, I plungèd in
And bade him follow. So indeed he did.
The torrent roared, and we did buffet it
With lusty sinews, throwing it aside
And stemming it with hearts of controversy.
But ere we could arrive the point proposed,
Caesar cried, “Help me, Cassius, or I sink!”
I, as Aeneas, our great ancestor,
Did from the flames of Troy upon his shoulder
The old Anchises bear, so from the waves of Tiber
Did I the tired Caesar. And this man
Is now become a god, and Cassius is
A wretched creature and must bend his body
If Caesar carelessly but nod on him.
He had a fever when he was in Spain,
And when the fit was on him, I did mark
How he did shake. 'Tis true, this god did shake!
His coward lips did from their color fly,
And that same eye whose bend doth awe the world
Did lose his luster. I did hear him groan,
Ay, and that tongue of his that bade the Romans
Mark him and write his speeches in their books—
“Alas,” it cried, “give me some drink, Titinius,”
As a sick girl. Ye gods, it doth amaze me
A man of such a feeble temper should
So get the start of the majestic world
And bear the palm alone.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
I know that virtue to be in you, Brutus,
As well as I do know your outward favor.
Well, honor is the subject of my story.
I cannot tell what you and other men
Think of this life, but, for my single self,
I had as lief not be as live to be
In awe of such a thing as I myself.
I was born free as Caesar. So were you.
We both have fed as well, and we can both
Endure the winter’s cold as well as he.
For once upon a raw and gusty day,
The troubled Tiber chafing with her shores,
Caesar said to me, “Darest thou, Cassius, now
Leap in with me into this angry flood
And swim to yonder point?” Upon the word,
Accoutred as I was, I plungèd in
And bade him follow. So indeed he did.
The torrent roared, and we did buffet it
With lusty sinews, throwing it aside
And stemming it with hearts of controversy.
But ere we could arrive the point proposed,
Caesar cried, “Help me, Cassius, or I sink!”
I, as Aeneas, our great ancestor,
Did from the flames of Troy upon his shoulder
The old Anchises bear, so from the waves of Tiber
Did I the tired Caesar. And this man
Is now become a god, and Cassius is
A wretched creature and must bend his body
If Caesar carelessly but nod on him.
He had a fever when he was in Spain,
And when the fit was on him, I did mark
How he did shake. 'Tis true, this god did shake!
His coward lips did from their color fly,
And that same eye whose bend doth awe the world
Did lose his luster. I did hear him groan,
Ay, and that tongue of his that bade the Romans
Mark him and write his speeches in their books—
“Alas,” it cried, “give me some drink, Titinius,”
As a sick girl. Ye gods, it doth amaze me
A man of such a feeble temper should
So get the start of the majestic world
And bear the palm alone.

A shout offstage. Trumpets play.
A shout offstage. Trumpets play.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
Another general shout!
I do believe that these applauses are
For some new honors that are heaped on Caesar.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
Why, man, he doth bestride the narrow world
Like a Colossus, and we petty men
Walk under his huge legs and peep about
To find ourselves dishonorable graves.
Men at some time are masters of their fates.
The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars
But in ourselves, that we are underlings.
Brutus and Caesar—what should be in that “Caesar?”
Why should that name be sounded more than yours?
Write them together, yours is as fair a name.
Sound them, it doth become the mouth as well.
Weigh them, it is as heavy. Conjure with 'em,
“Brutus” will start a spirit as soon as “Caesar.”
Now in the names of all the gods at once,
Upon what meat doth this our Caesar feed
That he is grown so great? Age, thou art shamed!
Rome, thou hast lost the breed of noble bloods!
When went there by an age, since the great flood,
But it was famed with more than with one man?
When could they say till now, that talked of Rome,
That her wide walks encompassed but one man?
Now is it Rome indeed, and room enough,
When there is in it but one only man.
Oh, you and I have heard our fathers say,
There was a Brutus once that would have brooked
Th' eternal devil to keep his state in Rome
As easily as a king.

That he is grown so great? Age, thou art shamed!
Rome, thou hast lost the breed of noble bloods!
When went there by an age, since the great flood,
But it was famed with more than with one man?
When could they say till now, that talked of Rome,
That her wide walks encompassed but one man?
Now is it Rome indeed, and room enough,
When there is in it but one only man.
Oh, you and I have heard our fathers say,
There was a Brutus once that would have brooked
Th' eternal devil to keep his state in Rome
As easily as a king.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
That you do love me, I am nothing jealous.
What you would work me to, I have some aim.
How I have thought of this and of these times
I shall recount hereafter. For this present,
I would not, so with love I might entreat you,
Be any further moved. What you have said
I will consider, what you have to say
I will with patience hear, and find a time
Both meet to hear and answer such high things.
Till then, my noble friend, chew upon this:
Brutus had rather be a villager
Than to repute himself a son of Rome
Under these hard conditions as this time
Is like to lay upon us.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
I am glad that my weak words
Have struck but thus much show of fire from Brutus.
CAESAR enters with his followers, including CASCA.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text: The games are done and Caesar is returning.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
As they pass by, pluck Casca by the sleeve,
And he will, after his sour fashion, tell you
What hath proceeded worthy note today.

sentences.speaker: CASSIUS
sentences.text:
As they pass by, pluck Casca by the sleeve,
And he will, after his sour fashion, tell you
What hath proceeded worthy note today.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
I will do so. But, look you, Cassius,
The angry spot doth glow on Caesar’s brow,
And all the rest look like a chidden train.
Calphurnia’s cheek is pale, and Cicero
Looks with such ferret and such fiery eyes
As we have seen him in the Capitol
Being crossed in conference by some senators.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
[aside to ANTONY] Let me have men about me that are
fat,
Sleek-headed men and such as sleep a-nights.
Yond Cassius has a lean and hungry look.
He thinks too much. Such men are dangerous.

sentences.speaker: ANTONY
sentences.text:
[aside to CAESAR] Fear him not, Caesar. He’s not
dangerous.
He is a noble Roman and well given.

sentences.speaker: ANTONY
sentences.text:
[aside to CAESAR] Fear him not, Caesar. He’s not
dangerous.
He is a noble Roman and well given.

sentences.speaker: ANTONY
sentences.text:
[aside to CAESAR] Fear him not, Caesar. He’s not
dangerous.
He is a noble Roman and well given.

sentences.speaker: CAESAR
sentences.text:
[aside to ANTONY] Would he were fatter! But I fear him
not.
Yet if my name were liable to fear,
I do not know the man I should avoid
So soon as that spare Cassius. He reads much.
He is a great observer, and he looks
Quite through the deeds of men. He loves no plays,
As thou dost, Antony . He hears no music.
Seldom he smiles, and smiles in such a sort
As if he mocked himself and scorned his spirit
That could be moved to smile at anything.
Such men as he be never at heart’s ease
Whiles they behold a greater than themselves,
And therefore are they very dangerous.
I rather tell thee what is to be feared
Than what I fear, for always I am Caesar.
Come on my right hand, for this ear is deaf,
And tell me truly what thou think’st of him.
